facts_disclosed: LSE degree, first in family to uni
Loves PwC, wants manager in 3 years

turns.content: so I need help thinking through something. I've been at my job for a bit over a year now and I want to map out where I'm going career-wise. like actually plan it properly instead of just surviving each week
That's a great instinct — moving from survival mode to intentional planning makes a big difference. What's the job and where do you see yourself heading?

I'm a junior accountant at PwC. and honestly I love it there. like yes the hours are long and busy season nearly killed me but the actual work is interesting, the training is class, and the brand on your CV is just different. PwC opens doors wallah
PwC is a great platform, and the fact that you genuinely enjoy the work — not just tolerating it for the CV line — puts you in a strong position. What level are you aiming for, and in what timeframe?

manager. I want to make manager in about 3 years. that might sound ambitious for someone whos only been there a year but I'm dead serious about it. the progression path at PwC is associate > senior associate > manager and I dont want to be someone who takes 6 years to get there

Three years to manager from junior level is ambitious but not unrealistic at PwC if you're getting strong reviews and hitting your milestones. What do you think the main hurdles are between where you are now and that manager title?

the ACA qualification is the obvious one. you need to pass all the exams. and then its about demonstrating leadership, building client relationships, being the person they trust to run engagements. I'm good at the technical side but the soft skills stuff is where I need to develop more. like being confident in rooms full of partners who are twice my age

The confidence piece is really common, especially early career. Those rooms of senior partners can be intimidating. But the fact that you can name it as a development area is half the battle. How's your relationship with your direct manager or mentor — do they know about your timeline?

my manager knows I'm ambitious. I dont think I've said '3 years to manager' out loud to her but she can tell I'm not here to coast. the thing is theres also this pressure from my family side. I did my degree at LSE — accounting — and I was the first person in my family to go to university. so theres this massive expectation that I'll come out the other side and like financially sort everyone out

Being the first in your family to go to university — and to LSE specifically, which is a top institution — that's an incredible achievement. But it sounds like it came with an unspoken contract: you made it, now you provide. That's a lot of weight to carry alongside your own career ambitions.

thats exactly what it is. an unspoken contract. nobody explicitly says 'you need to give us money' but when you're the one who went to LSE and got the Big 4 job and everyone else is still figuring things out... you just feel it. and I want to help my family, I genuinely do. but sometimes the pressure makes the career stuff feel less like MY dream and more like something I owe people

That tension between personal ambition and family obligation is real, and it's worth being honest with yourself about. Because the two can coexist — you can love your career AND want to support your family — but if it starts feeling purely like obligation, you'll burn out. The fact that you said you love PwC and the work is interesting tells me it IS your dream too. The family pressure is on top of that, not instead of it.

yeah you're right. like when I strip away all the family expectations and just think about ME and what I want — I still want to be at PwC. I still want to make manager. the training opportunities here are mad, the exit opportunities if I ever leave are even better, and honestly a girl from where I'm from making manager at PwC? that would mean everything

That's powerful. And that last part — the representation angle — is something that genuinely matters, both for you and for people who come after you. Someone from your background making manager at PwC sends a signal. Now, for the practical side: to hit manager in 3 years, you'll want to be strategic. A few things to think about:

1. Tell your manager your timeline explicitly. Don't hint — say "I want to make manager in 3 years, what do I need to do?"
2. Seek out visible projects. Manager promotions at Big 4 firms weight client impact and leadership heavily.
3. Build relationships with partners beyond your direct team. You mentioned struggling in rooms with them — start small, even one-on-one coffees.
4. Nail the ACA exams first time. Resits slow the timeline.

the ACA thing is so real. I cannot afford to fail any of those exams. every resit is basically a 6 month delay. and the idea of telling my manager the explicit timeline is scary but you're right, if I dont say it nobody's going to plan it for me

Exactly. Managers can't advocate for you in promotion conversations if they don't know what you're aiming for. And most managers at PwC respect ambition — they got there by being ambitious themselves. The worst that can happen is she says "that's aggressive, here's what you'd need to do" and then you have a roadmap. As for the ACA exams, do you have a study plan in place?

sort of. PwC gives us study leave which is good but during busy season you're basically studying at midnight after a 14 hour day which is not ideal loool. I need to be more disciplined about it tbh. maybe block out proper study windows and treat them like client meetings that cant be moved

That's a solid approach — if it's in your calendar as a non-negotiable block, people are less likely to schedule over it. And treating study time with the same seriousness as client work is the right mindset. You wouldn't skip a client meeting because you're tired, so don't skip study either. When's your next exam sitting?

not till March so I've got time but it'll fly by. okay I feel like I have a clearer picture now. step one: have the explicit conversation with my manager about the 3 year timeline. step two: get serious about ACA study blocks. step three: start putting myself in rooms with partners even when it's uncomfortable. I'm all in wallah

That's a clean action plan. And the fact that you're this intentional about your career a year in puts you ahead of most people at your level. Go have that conversation with your manager — I think you'll be surprised how well it lands. Good luck with it.
